title: The Use of Robotics in Military Surgery: A Scoping Review Protocol
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"lc3oh","text":"Military surgical care is delivered across highly variable environments, including forward operating bases, battlefield settings, naval vessels, aeromedical evacuation platforms, and deployed field hospitals. These austere and resource-constrained environments pose logistical, ethical, and clinical challenges.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"csobi","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"htnmm","text":"Robotic surgical systems—such as teleoperated, semi-autonomous, or autonomous robotic platforms—have been increasingly integrated into civilian surgical practice. These systems have demonstrated benefits including improved dexterity, enhanced visualization, and minimally invasive capabilities.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vwsve","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"umrtq","text":"Within military contexts, robotics may offer additional advantages, including:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"g3wj3","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"zv2a6","text":"- Remote telesurgical intervention in conflict affected settings","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"qnx8u","text":"- Reducing exposure of surgical personnel to hostile environments","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"w0b2l","text":"- Augmentation of limited surgical expertise in forward locations","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3nmm2","text":"- Potential future integration with autonomous evacuation or battlefield systems","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"16b8l","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"y1pnh","text":"Despite growing interest in robotic-assisted and telesurgical systems for defence medicine, the scope, maturity, applications, limitations, and research gaps in military-specific surgical robotics remain unclear. No comprehensive scoping review has yet mapped the breadth of literature in this domain.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"oikfd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"zpxd5","text":"This scoping review aims to systematically map existing evidence on the use of robotics in military surgical settings, identify research gaps, and inform future research and policy directions.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Primary Objective: To map and characterize the extent, range, and nature of published literature on the use of robotics in military surgical care.
To categorise types of robotic technologies used in military surgery.
To summarize reported outcomes, feasibility, limitations, and ethical considerations.
To identify research gaps and future directions.
What robotic systems have been described in military surgical contexts?
In what operational environments have robotic systems been proposed or implemented?
What types of surgical procedures are reported?
What outcomes (clinical, logistical, operational) are described?
What barriers and facilitators to implementation are identified?
This scoping review will follow the methodological framework developed by Arksey and O'Malley and refined by Levac. Reporting will adhere to the PRISMA-ScR checklist.
AI-integrated robotic surgery in military contexts
Primary research studies (quantitative, qualitative, mixed methods)
Military medical doctrine or policy reports
Civilian-only robotic surgery with no military application
Firm base or planned surgical contexts with no direct operational or deployed context relevance.
The following databases will be searched:
Pubmed Medline on EBSCO platform CINAHL on EBSCO platform EMBASE on Ovid platform
Reference lists of included studies will be screened for additional sources.
A comprehensive search strategy has been developed with a medical librarian to search:

Search strategies will be adapted for each database. No date restrictions will initially be applied. There is no language restriction and non-English language publications will be translated using google translate where possible.
All identified citations will be imported into a reference management software and duplicates removed.
Screening will occur in two stages:
Two independent reviewers will screen studies at each stage. Discrepancies will be resolved through discussion.
A PRISMA flow diagram will document the selection process.
A standardized data extraction form will be developed and pilot-tested. Extracted data will include:
A descriptive analytical approach will be used.
Numerical summaries (e.g., frequency of study types, countries)
Thematic analysis (e.g., operational feasibility, ethical concerns, technical challenges)
Conceptual mapping of technology maturity levels
No formal risk-of-bias assessment will be conducted, consistent with scoping review methodology.
Ethical approval is not required as this review uses publicly available literature.
Conference presentation in military or surgical forums
Policy briefing to defence health stakeholders
Provide a comprehensive mapping of robotics in military surgery
Identify maturity gaps between civilian and military applications
Highlight technological, logistical, and ethical challenges
Inform future research, procurement decisions, and doctrinal development
creator: John Whitaker
